---
title: |
  Penalized for Faith: Muslim Bias in Economic Behavioral Games Across 25 EU Countries
subtitle: |
  Evidence from a Conjoint Survey Experiment
abstract: |
  We reanalyize data
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  eval: true
  include: true
draft: true
draft-mode: visible
---

## Introduction {#sec-introduction}

Numerous studies have documented a significant ‘Muslim penalty’ across European societies [@helbling2014opposing; @helbling2012islamophobia]. In labour markets, housing, and everyday social interaction, individuals identified as Muslim are often disadvantaged relative to otherwise comparable non-Muslims. Yet establishing the existence of this penalty, i.e. the systematic disadvantage incurred by individuals identified as Muslim, is only the starting point for a substantive understanding of the phenomenon. The more demanding questions concern who discriminates, against whom, and under which conditions.

Against this background, this paper revisits a large-scale conjoint survey experiment conducted across 25 European Union member states by @hahm2024divided. Using a combination of dictator and trust games alongside a conjoint design, they estimate the relative strength of multiple social divides. While their main focus is on the dominance of the partisan divide, their analysis also identifies a notable anti-Muslim bias that is consistent across all 25 countries. Whenever Muslim identity is experimentally signalled, respondents’ allocation decisions reveal a systematic penalty relative to otherwise comparable non-Muslim profiles. What this finding does not reveal, however, is whether and how the magnitude of this penalty varies with the other attributes attached to Muslim profiles, with the characteristics and political orientations of respondents, or with the interaction between profiles and respondents.

This paper takes that heterogeneity as its central object of inquiry. Treating the Muslim penalty as the primary quantity of interest, it shifts from the AMCE framework towards a Conditional Average Treatment Effect logic and distinguishes two sources of variation [@robinson2024detect]. The first concerns whether the remaining attributes of the displayed Muslim profile, such as class, nationality, or EU stance, moderate the magnitude of the penalty. The second, and substantively more consequential, concerns whether individual-level characteristics of respondents, in particular their populist predispositions and their orientations towards European integration, account for who discriminates more strongly. That the penalty is unlikely to constitute a single, undifferentiated response is suggested both by evidence that hostility towards Muslims is driven less by nominal religious affiliation than by perceived religiosity [@helbling2020islamophobia; @helbling2022muslim] and by the contextual, social-identity logic underlying the original study [@hahm2024divided].

Methodologically, the study employs Bayesian Causal Forests with nested random intercepts [@hahn2020bayesian] to recover heterogeneous treatment effects in a data-driven manner, cross-validated against a frequentist generalised random forest [@athey2019generalized]. Our results suggest that there is substantial heterogeneity in the depth of the penalty, but almost none in its presence. With barely any exceptions, the demographics, attitudes and profile attributes of respondents shift the magnitude of anti-Muslim bias without nullifying or even reversing it. The strongest moderation is found across orientations towards European integration, where respondents opposed to or sceptical of the EU display systematically larger penalties than their pro-European counterparts, although a clear penalty persists even among the latter.

In a broader context, the study promotes a more nuanced understanding of anti-Muslim discrimination in Europe. By situating the variation in penalties within the cosmopolitan–populist divide that defines contemporary European politics, it suggests that the bias is less an isolated individual disposition than a politically driven phenomenon.  Understanding the conditions that amplify this bias is crucial for grasping the mechanisms that sustain it and the potential avenues for its mitigation.


{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#fig-diff-in-means >}}

## Research Design {#sec-design}

### Data and Experimental Setting

Our data structure is inherently hierarchical.
Each respondent played three rounds of a Dictator (or Solidarity) game and a Trust game, the latter introduced announced reciprocity in the instructions.
Additionally, given the cross-national structure, respondents are grouped into 25 countries.

In each round, respondents received a fictional co-player with randomized attributes age, gender, social class, nationality, partisan affiliation, EU stance and religion.
Randomization occured in multiple instances: both the order in which attributes were displayed as well as the *feature* of each attribute were both random by design.

To adress the issue of implausible co-player configurations and in light of their research questions, @hahm2024divided implemented four distinct treatment conditions throughout the behavioral games.
In condition one, the conjoint co-player was displayed as a conational, with EU stance and partisan affiliation.
Condition two included conationals with partisan affiliation but no EU stance.
Condition three showed a different but EU nationality and the EU stance attribute, while the final condition four showed only some nationality with no partisan affiliation or EU stance.

Importantly, the religion attribute, consisting the of four features *muslim*, *catholic*, *protestant* and *non-religious* was shown across all of the above mentioned conditions.
This allows us to utilize the full (game) dataset for estimating the Muslim penalty.
One caveat is constituted by the fact that there is not strict control condition for the religion attribute, i.e., in which there was *no* religion feature shown at all.
We refrain from selecting *non-religious* as a substnative control condition as being non-religious itself might not constitute a neutral baseline as it might for instance be perceived differently in eastern European countries with post-communist legacies than elsewhere.
To circumvent the issues with selecting a reference category [see @leeper2020measuring] and to simplify the causal terminology, we collapse the religion feature into a binary treatment indicator $T$ with $T=1$ if the profile was displayed as Muslim and $T=0$ otherwise.[^1]
This choice is motivated by our substantive interest in studying heterogeneity in the *Muslim penalty*, not analyzing the conjoint allocation game with its full multidimensional treatment space.

[^1]: This approach effectively averages the non-Muslim religion feature against the Muslim one. As we are primarily interested in drivers of Muslim bias, not certain facets of Muslim bias itself, we opt for this simpler approach.


### Causal Estimand

While our analysis builds on the conjoint survey experiment fielded by
[@hahm2023divided; @hahm2024divided], our research question differs from theirs.
Original study focuses on the identity side of the story and how six different attributes of it, including religion, affect token allocations.
Since we focus exclusively on the Muslim penalty and ask what drives variation in it across individuals and contexts, it also changes the quantities of interest.
The conventional estimand in conjoint experiments is the Average Marginal Component Effect [AMCE, for definition see @hainmueller2014causal].
However, since it by definition is the average, it conceals the heterogeneity in how different respondents respond to the same treatment, which is exactly what we are interested in. Therefore, following @robinson2024detect, we adopt the idea of nested causal quantities.

Firstly, as discussed above, we simplify the religion attribute.
After this simplification, our treatment variable is binary: $T_{ric} \in \{0,1\}$, where $T_{ric} = 1$ if the profile is displayed as Muslim and 0 otherwise.
This change shifts our estimand from the AMCE framework to a standard average treatment effect logic.
We observe only the realized outcome, which can be represented in potential outcomes notation as following:

$$
Y_{ric}^{g,\text{obs}} =
T_{ric} \cdot Y_{ric}^{g}(1) + (1 - T_{ric}) \cdot Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
$$ {#eq-observed}

Since both games reflect different types of discrimination, we estimate separate models for each game $g \in \{D, TR\}$, where $D$ denotes the dictator game and $TR$ the trust game. Our baseline estimand for each game is the Average Treatment Effect (ATE):\text{ATE}^{g} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)\right]
$${#eq-ate}

\noindent where $Y_{ric}^{g}(t)$ is the number of tokens allocated by respondent $i$ in country $c$ during round $r$ of game $g$ under treatment assignment $t$.
This quantity captures the average Muslim penalty in the target population. Since the religion attribute is randomly assigned by design, $\{Y_{ric}^{g}(1),\, Y_{ric}^{g}(0)\}  \perp T_{ric}$.
However, as the sample was recruited to be representative of national populations only in terms of age and gender [@hahm2024divided, p. 73], we adopt the more conservative Sample ATE (SATE), which conditions on the observed sample rather than making broader claims about the target population:[^2]

$$
    \text{SATE}^{g} =
        \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i}
        \mathbb{E}_{r}\!\left[Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)\right]
$${#eq-sate}

As we are interested in factors that explain the heterogeneity in  Muslim penalty,  we decompose the SATE into its conditional counterpart, the Conditional Average Treatment Effect (CATE):

$$
    \tau^{g}(X_{ic},\, A_{ric}) =
        \mathbb{E}\!\left[
            Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
            \;\middle|\;
            X_{ic},\, A_{ric}
        \right]
$${#eq-cate1}

\noindent where $X_{ic}$ denotes time-invariant respondent-level covariates, which includes HERE NEED TO ADD WHICH PRE-TREATMENT VARIABLES WE USE, and $A_{ric}$ denotes the remaining profile attributes displayed in round $r$, namely gender, age, social class, party affiliation and nationality.
Importantly, we do not treat these profile attributes as separate treatments as in the AMCE approach, but as potential moderators of the Muslim penalty.

This single CATE function captures two different sources of heterogeneity.
First, we are interested in *causal moderation*, namely whether and how respondent-level characteristics shape the size of the Muslim penalty.
Disaggregating to the individual level by averaging over rounds, we define the respondent-level CATE:

$$
    \tau_{ic}^{g}
        = \mathbb{E}_{r}\!\left[\tau^{g}(X_{ic},\, A_{ric})\right]
        = \mathbb{E}\!\left[
            Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
            \;\middle|\;
            X_{ic}
          \right]
$${#eq-cate-respondent}

Second, we are interested in *causal interaction*: whether other profile attributes change the Muslim penalty.
Disaggregating to the profile level by averaging over respondents, we define the profile-level CATE:[^3]

$$
    \tau_{ric}^{g}
        = \mathbb{E}_{i}\!\left[\tau^{g}(X_{ic},\, A_{ric})\right]
        = \mathbb{E}\!\left[
            Y_{ric}^{g}(1) - Y_{ric}^{g}(0)
            \;\middle|\;
            A_{ric}
          \right]
$${#eq-cate-profile}

These quantities are linked through the law of iterated expectations, which ensures internal consistency across levels of the hierarchy: $\text{SATE}^{g} = \mathbb{E}_{i}\!\left[\mathbb{E}_{r}[\tau^{g}(X_{ic}, A_{ric})]\right] = \mathbb{E}_{i}[\tau_{ic}^{g}]$.

[^2]: Since we are interested in treatment effect heterogeneity and not the average penalty itself, the choice between ATE and SATE is inconsequential for the main analysis.

[^3]:The subscripts $r, i, c$ reflect the hierarchical structure of the outcome $Y_{ric}^g$ (round $r$, nested in respondent $i$, nested in country $c$) and not the conditioning set in the expectation. We apply the same convention to the covariates and the disaggregated CATEs: $X_{ic}$ carries the country index because respondent $i$ is nested in country $c$ (so $c$ is determined by $i$), even though country is not part of the conditioning set; likewise $\tau_{ric}^g$ retains $i$ after marginalizing over respondents. After marginalizing over respondents, only $A_{ric}$ remains as the conditioning argument; after marginalizing over rounds, only $X_{ic}$ remains.


### Estimation Strategy

- one paragraph (at most) on history/development of non-parametric approaches to treatment heterogeneity analysis/causal inference
    - random forest approach
        - one "branch" developed RF into BARTs and later BCF
        - another "branch" used sample splitting ML technique (double machine learning)
        - recent advances in treat. hetero. analyses also in political science and conjoint specifically: 
            - @robinson2024detect use BARTs which suffer from general BART limitations [@hahn2020bayesian]
            - @goplerud2025estimating Bayesian mixture of regularized logistic regressions for multi-dimensional treatment 
                - not used as we are theoretically interested in Muslim penalty, not full multidimensional treatment, and their method is made for bernoulli family forced choice (classical) conjoint outcomes, which differs from @hahm2024divided's setting
        - pro's and con's of each: cite @ratkovic2021subgroup for direct comparison of sample splitting ML and BARTs
            - under randomization, both perform relatively equal
            - without (i.e. with non-random treatment), sample splitting approach superior (although @hahn2020bayesian's addresses the first concern stated by @ratkovic2021subgroup, namely that BARTs "[do] not model the treatment as a function of the covariates", the second concern [using the full sample for both model and outcome estimation] remains real in the BCA extension) 
        

- our approach: @athey2019generalized Causal Forest primary and Bayesian Causal Forest for robustness
    - initial problem: inherent hierarchical data structure violates IID assumptions
    - CF allows for clustered SEs, BCF for random intercepts -> neither for nested multiple levels
    - on the other hand, we are not interested in comparative country comparisons, just want to account for the sample characteristics
    - idea: CF with respondent clustered SEs (to account for multiple rows per respondent) and country fixed effects in the covariate matrix (to account for multiple countries in sample), and BCF with nested random intercepts (building upon code used by @yeager2022synergistic)
    - tested both estimators with synthetic data (with similar hierarchical structure): CF slightly more precise in test data, BCF correctly recovered random intercepts
    - but nested RI structure unfeasable on whole sample due to computational / temporal limitations (add footnote explaining technical reasons), hence BCF for robustness only fitted with country RI (and respondent uncertainty incorporated in post-fitting stage via bootstrapping)
    - Hence: present CF results, BCF in appendix as robustness


--- OLD
Given the hierarchical data structure, causal forest [@wager2018estimation; @athey2019estimating] approaches are not optimal, as they assume iid observations and thus potentially return narrower standard errors than appropriate.
Simulation by @ratkovic2021subgroup revealed BART and GRF perform comparable under random treatment assignment.
We therefore implement Bayesian causal forests (BCT) with randomc intercepts for account for the nested structure in our data. 
@hahn2020bayesian extend Bayesian additive regression trees (BART) [@chipman2010bart; @chipman2006bayesian] to BCF by separating modelling of prognostic and treatment effects, thereby reducing regularization-induced confounding [@prevot2025hierarchical]. 
While themselves also requireing independent observations, recent implementations further extended BCF to allow for the inclusion of both random intercepts [@yeager2022synergistic] and random slopes [@yeager2019national], allowing to account for group-level structures in the underlying data.
We adopt this approach for our study to identify treatment heterogeneity in a data-driven way, while accounting for the nested structure using random intercepts and utilizing the full posterior uncertainty quantification for our treatment effect estimates.

On the other hand, GRF apply an "honest" double machine learning approach [@chernozhukov2018double], where forest structure is estimated separately from point and uncertainty estimated.

We therefore pursue a dual strategy to increase robustness of our heterogeneity analysis.
First, we extend the BCF with random intercepts by @yeager2022synergistic to nested random intercepts, to properly account for our hierarchical data structure with outcomes nested in respondents, which are themselves nested in countries.
Second, we implement a frequentist causal forest [@athey2019generalized] with country fixed effects and respondent level clusters to enhance robustness across estimation procedures.
In both cases, we estimate separate forests for each game.
Consequently, we retreive four main models, BCF and CF for the dictator and trust game.

*Working notes on the methodological argument:*

- Bayesian causal forests [@hahn2020bayesian] are our primary estimator: they target the treatment-effect surface $\tau(x)$ directly with a separate prior, avoiding the regularization-induced confounding that afflicts BART-then-difference approaches [@green2012modeling]; the continuous token-allocation outcome is the natural, strong case for the method; and the Bayesian posterior delivers credible intervals on individual CATEs and on any functional of $\tau$ for free.
- Nested random intercepts (the Yeager application lineage: [@yeager2019national; @yeager2022synergistic]) enter on $\mu(x)$ only, as a nuisance correction for the cross-national and repeated-measures structure; country is not interpreted substantively, and the documented fallback is country-only random intercepts plus cluster-bootstrapped respondent uncertainty if the dense $W^{\top}W$ solve proves too costly.
- A `grf` causal forest [@athey2019generalized] supplies cross-framework robustness: `clusters = respondent_id`, country fixed effects in $X$, and a best linear projection onto the same frozen moderators; agreement across the two frameworks is the robustness story, while disagreement is flagged.
- We do not use Goplerud's estimator [@goplerud2025estimating]: it is designed for heterogeneity of a high-dimensional treatment under a forced-choice Bernoulli model, so collapsing to a binary Muslim treatment with a continuous outcome makes its machinery mismatched and unnecessary — we cite it as related work, not as a method we run.
- We also do not run Robinson and Duch's `cjbart` [@robinson2024detect] as an estimator: it is conceptually aligned, and we borrow its nested-estimand vocabulary (observation-, round-, and individual-level marginal component effects) to organize profile-level versus respondent-level heterogeneity, but its BART-then-aggregate construction is exactly the RIC-prone approach BCF improves on, so it is not a third fit.
- A terminology clarification: in this literature "nested" usually denotes nested *estimands* [@robinson2024detect], whereas our random intercepts address the nested *sampling structure* — a separate concern.
- The conjoint design supplies a known treatment propensity that is passed directly to the estimators; throughout, the analysis is descriptive and exploratory, with no causal claims beyond CATE estimation.
--- OLDEND

The CF recovered an overall estimated SATE of -0.5641  (SE 0.0190);  95% CI [-0.6013, -0.5268] in the dictator and Trust ATE: -0.6419  (SE 0.0205);  95% CI [-0.6820, -0.6018] in the trust game.
As this corresponds to the simple difference in means presented in the beginning, we interpret this as a first "passed" check, as the forest recover the the correct overall SATEs.(Footnote on BCF SATE estimates)
@fig-grf-combined-toc in the appendix shows the targeting operator characeristic (TOC) curves for our causal forest fits of both games.
Both indicate presence of heterogenous treatment effects in our data, with the area under the curve (AUTOC) being significantly larger than 0.


We present our main results by using the visual strategy employed by @green2012modeling, i.e., estimated CATEs as a function of our moderator variables @woody2021model.
This allows us to determine how changes in one moderator, holding others constant (CHECK IF THATS CORRECT), affect the Muslim penalty in our games.
Should there be non-linear trends, the Causal Forest should recover this.
For categorical variables, we estimate the point interval and honest 95% confidence intervals for each level, plotted as shapes and line segments, respectively.
For continuous (including scale ratings), we obtain point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for each value of the scale (for age and year of education we chose a prespecified number of "bins" for which to obtain the estimates) and connect those, yielding line and ribbons to visualize trends.
As we estimate separate models for the two types of games, we present each with different coloring within the same plot -- divergences would indicate different hetergoeneity effects across both games.

## Empirical Analysis {#sec-analysis}


{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-prof >}}

- Interpretations: 
    - overall, Muslim penalty remains stable with regards to other profile attributes
    - e.g. age, sex, and eu position
    - across all attribute dimensions trust game yields consistently lower CATE point estimates, but intervals overlap with those from dictator game.
        - only exception: Muslim profiles presented as non Eu nationals receive significantly fewer (i.e. the penatly is significantly larger) in trust game
        - though interestingly this difference arises more from an upwards shift of the Dictator non-eu estimate
    - across both games, Muslim profiles presented as upper class are penalized slightly less


{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-resp-1 >}}

- Interpretations:
    - Gender: males penaluze more than female respondents across both games, but gender gap only significant in Dictator game
    - Religion: strongest bias from catholic and protestant respondents, weakest bias (judged by point estimates) by orthodox respondents
    - class: dont-know respondents show smallest bias across both games (but also widest intervals), significant difference between solidarity and trust for upper middle class respondents, point estimates suggest slighly lower penalty for working class in dictator, but not systematic
    - Residence type: slightly larger penalty from respondents in rural areas compared to large city folks, difference more systematic in trust game, small/mid town in between
    - Attachement to country: seems negatively related, although hard to judge as very few denied attachement in the first place (hence the super wide intervals)
    - attachement to EU: almost linearly associated with reduction in Muslim bias
    - attachement to Europe: less clear than EU attachement, uncertainty bands allow weakly positive (dictator) or flat (trust) trend interpretation
    - Can understand EU politics: no indication of moderation
    - Officials talk, not act: more agreement, more bias across both games
    - Good vs evil: seems to be negative trend from 3 to 6 but it seems also to bounce off / turn flat toward end of scale, unlike pop_reps
    - Compromise is selling out: again clear negative trend, the more agreement, the larger the Muslim penalty
    - Compromise is important: if at all, more agreement associated with slightly less penalty, but overall strong levels of agreement in sample (hence few observations with <= 4)
    - Age: for younger respondents (<30 years) uncertainty intervals "scratch" zero (no Muslim bias) more often and for youngest age (18) the point estimates even surpass zero, the only instance where we observe that happen, then distance between upper bound and zero seems to increase, indicating a negative trend up to age 65, where the upper bounds seem to rise again. This might indicate a U shape relationship betwen age and Muslim penalty, but it might also mechanically arise from the smaller sample size for 65+ (and thus increased uncertainty bands)

{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-resp-2 >}}

- Interpretations:
    - listen to other groups: same as "Compromise is important", maybe for the right/upper half of the scale even slighlty stronger positive trend
    - Leave decisions to experts: for values <=3 very few obs, in range 4 to 7 negative trend in trust game, less so in dictator
    - leaders above ordinary citizens: as previous item, at most small increase (trust more visible) in bias with higher agreement, very few observations <=3
    - parties do more harm: across both games, more agreement -> larger bias
    - ordinary people dont know: pretty close to "stable" effect here
    - EU should pursue long term goals: more agreement, less bias across both games, though slope flattens the from 5 to 7
    - Eu should heed the people: more agreement, more bias
    - EU effect on economy (x axis to be flipped): the more negative the evaluation, the higher the Muslim penalty
    - EU effeect on culture/identity: the more negative the evaluation, the larger Muslim penalty
    - EU effect on political status: again more negative evaluations more Muslim bias
    - better to abolish EU: higher agreement, larger Muslim penalty
    - satisfaction with EU: the reverse, higher agrement yields lower Muslim penalty 
    - Age left education: pretty flat trend

- Overall: Overall we find substantial heterogeneity across respondent subgroups, demographics and pre-treatment attitudes, but with very few exception these heterogeneities relate to the extend of anit-Muslim bias, not the presence of it. Even in those very few exceptions, it is only the upper uncertainty bounds that just scratch the zero line, no (single) moderator seems to credibly nullify, let alone reverse, the (negative) Muslim effect on token allocation. 
Strongest moderation heterogeneity is found across EU evaluations/attitudes, where the pattern in the data generally suggest that respondents against EU integration or with negative evaluations of the EU show systematically higher levels of anti-Muslim bias than those favoring / positively evaluating the EU and its effects. Still, even in the pro-European sides of the scales, robust Muslim penalties persist, albeit at lower levels. 
This impression is supported by the variable importance plot in the appendix (@fig-grf-combined-vi), which identifies the mentioned EU items as 4 of the 5 most "important" variables.

## Robustness {#sec-robustness}

The results based on the Bayesian Causal Forest [@hahn2020bayesian] yield identical patterns for the conjoint profile level moderators (@fig-bcf-combined-prof in the appendix).

The same picture appears for the individual level covariates. 
Although uncertainty bounds appear smaller for small-N levels / values, possibly a consequence of the "lack" of "honest" sample splitting approach, the substantive trends described in the results section seem to replicate.
At most, the moderation effects of continuous survey items appear more "smooth" in the BCF estimates, but direction and trends remain -- Muslim penalty is overall robust, there appears a small gender gap (though less so in the trust game estimates), EU related items show strongest heterogeneity differences across scales, and the same  populism items as described above are associated with differing magnitudes of Muslim penalty.
The only notable disagreement appears in the recovered heterogeneity of respondents' age.
In the BCF results there is no "touching" the zero effect visible for younger respondents, neither does the trend approach zero in larger values of age as described in the lose u turn interpretation above.


## Limitations {#sec-limits}

Only looked at within covariate moderating heterogeneity, not cross-covariate interactions.
Focused on Muslim penalty, implicitly assuming this is primary signal respondents perceive in experiment, this might not be the case in reality.
To avoid potential post-treatment biases, we only focuses on pre-treatment covariates (expection being years of education and residence type). 
This led us to exclude theoretically / potentially interesting variables such as general left right placement, EU integration placement, nativist attitudes and more which future endeaouvors might shed more light on, incorporating systematic ways to assess these as well. 

## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

\singlespacing

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

## Appendix {.unnumbered}

### Appendix A {.unnumbered}

#### Examplary Profile From Conjoint Experiment {.unnumbered}

![Player 2 Profile Example. *Note:* Figure embedded from @hahm2024divided, Figure 1.](https://static.cambridge.org/binary/version/id/urn:cambridge.org:id:binary:20240123150926403-0273:S0003055423000266:S0003055423000266_fig1.png)

#### Distribution of Outcome Variable {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#fig-outcome-distr >}}

#### Distribution of Profile-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#tbl-balance-round-mods >}}

#### Distribution of Respondent-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#tbl-balance-resp-mods >}}

#### Question Wordings and Scales {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/09_additional_figs_tables.qmd#tbl-wordings >}}

### Appendix B Additional CF Figures {.unnumbered}

#### TOC / AUTOC Curve {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-toc >}}

<!-- #### Best Linear Projection {.unnumbered} -->

<!-- {{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-blp >}} -->

#### Variable Importance {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/07_postprocess_grf.qmd#fig-grf-combined-vi >}}

### Appendix C Main Results from BCF Method {.unnumbered}

#### BCF Moderation by Profile-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/08_postprocess_bcf.qmd#fig-bcf-combined-prof >}}

#### BCF Moderation by Respondent-level Covariates {.unnumbered}

{{< embed code/08_postprocess_bcf.qmd#fig-bcf-combined-resp-1 >}}

{{< embed code/08_postprocess_bcf.qmd#fig-bcf-combined-resp-2 >}}
